rxn_class_summary: Metal-free electrochemical dihydroxylation of unactivated alkenes using Et4NI/NH4I as iodide mediators and water as the oxygen source, proceeding via in-situ generated iodine and an epoxide intermediate
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of iodide (I-) to iodine radical/I2, which acts as the alkene-activating mediator; anode also oxidizes Et3N (generated from Et4N+ decomposition) to an α-aminoalkyl radical that abstracts iodine from an alkyl iodide intermediate; cathode evolves H2

key_intermediates.name: iodine (I2) generated from anodic oxidation of I-
β-iodo alcohol (Int-II / compound 4) from water addition to iodonium
α-aminoalkyl radical from electrooxidation of Et3N
β-hydroxy carbon radical (Int-III) after I-atom abstraction
epoxide intermediate (Int-IV / compound 3i) from intramolecular cyclization/ring closure

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes I- to I• which dimerizes to I2; I2 forms an iodonium with the alkene that is opened by H2O to give a β-iodo alcohol (4). In parallel, the anode oxidizes Et3N (from Et4N+) to an α-aminoalkyl radical that abstracts the C-I of 4, giving a carbon radical that loses an electron and cyclizes to an epoxide (3i); H2O then opens the epoxide to the diol (2a), while the cathode reduces protons to H2.

probe_reagents.name: H2 18-O (isotope labeling)
probe_reagents.observation: Product 2a incorporates 18O in both hydroxyl groups (HRMS m/z 299.1392 vs calc 299.1390), showing both OH groups come from water

probe_reagents.name: I2 (stoichiometric, no electricity)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction of 1a with I2 in the dark without current gave iodo-alcohols 4 (21%) and 4' (9%), supporting I2 as the alkene-activating mediator

probe_reagents.name: isolated β-iodo alcohol 4 (stoichiometric intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: Under condition A, 4 gave 2a in 82%; without electricity 4 was recovered (93%), confirming 4 as a key on-cycle intermediate requiring current

probe_reagents.name: isolated epoxide 3i (stoichiometric intermediate)
probe_reagents.observation: 3i gave 2a in 85% under condition A; without electricity 3i was recovered (95%), confirming epoxide as downstream intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Omission of Et4NI; use NH4I/TFA only
probe_reagents.observation: No 2a formed and 4 recovered (92%), indicating Et4N+ (source of Et3N) is required to convert 4 onward

probe_reagents.name: Et3N added to 4 with current
probe_reagents.observation: 2a obtained in 27% with current; no product without electricity, confirming electrooxidation of tertiary amine to α-aminoalkyl radical is required for C-I activation

probe_reagents.name: Et4NBF4 (non-iodide electrolyte) with 4 under current
probe_reagents.observation: Time-course showed 4 decreasing, 3i rising then falling, 2a increasing — demonstrating sequential 4 → 3i → 2a conversion

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of Et4NI at variable scan rates
probe_reagents.observation: Linear ip vs v^1/2 plots (R2 = 0.9753, 0.9983) indicate diffusion-controlled oxidation of iodide

notes: Both hydroxyl oxygens of the diol originate from water (18O labeling). The mechanism invokes a dual mediator system: iodide for alkene activation and in-situ-generated Et3N (from Et4N+ decomposition) for halogen atom transfer via an α-aminoalkyl radical. The epoxide (Int-IV) and an iodo-cyclic oxocarbenium-type Int-III are proposed to be in equilibrium, with water opening to give the diol. GC-MS detection of Et3N supports the amine-mediator hypothesis.
